items.title: Lately, I have been focusing on creating practical examples of how AI can be integ…
items.excerpt:
Lately, I have been focusing on creating practical examples of how AI can be integrated into analytics workflows. This is not about slides or strategy documents; it's about developing tools that allow users to ask questions and receive answers directly.

One such example is a Retail Q&A Assistant built using Streamlit and Databricks. The concept is straightforward: a user poses a natural language question related to retail data. The app validates that the question is relevant to retail analytics, employs a Databricks Foundation Model to generate SQL, executes that SQL against Unity Catalog tables, and then presents the results as visualizations or data tables.

What I find most significant is not merely the translation from natural language to SQL. Many demos have accomplished that. The more

items.title: I think we’ve been centering our data platforms around the wrong thing.
items.excerpt:
I think we’ve been centering our data platforms around the wrong thing.

For years, the database has been the thing we protected, tuned, documented, and celebrated. We invested countless hours optimizing indexes, partitioning tables, reducing query times, and carefully modeling schemas. When someone asked about the health of the platform, we talked about the database.

Working with Databricks has challenged that way of thinking.

The database is becoming an implementation detail.

The real product is the pipeline.

Not just the ingestion, but the entire lifecycle. How data arrives. How it’s validated. How quality checks are enforced. How transformations are versioned. How failures are recovered. How lineage is captured. How the entire process can be recreated six months from now without someone digging through old documentation or trying

items.title: Too much of our effort goes into moving data instead of helping people make bett…
items.excerpt:
Too much of our effort goes into moving data instead of helping people make better decisions and that overhead is baked into how enterprise analytics has been built. Data is constantly extracted, transformed, copied, reconciled, validated, modeled, and served across systems, creating complexity that slows everything down.

Over time, I stopped asking whether it was necessary and started asking whether we’d just become comfortable with it.

Look at a typical enterprise architecture diagram. Most of it isn’t solving business problems it’s solving the problems created by moving data. We build pipelines because applications, analytics, and AI all live in different places, each needing its own copy.

That’s why Lakebase from Databricks caught my attention.

Not because it’s PostgreSQL.
Not because it’s another database.

Because it challenges an assumption many of us

items.title: The AI Question No One Wants to Answer: When Do You Stop Renting Intelligence?
items.excerpt:
The AI Question No One Wants to Answer: When Do You Stop Renting Intelligence?

At some point, every serious AI conversation is going to shift from “which LLM should we buy?” to “which parts of intelligence do we need to control?”

We have seen this pattern before with data warehouses, cloud platforms, integration engines, cybersecurity tools, and analytics infrastructure. We start with speed and convenience. Usage spreads. The bill gets real. The data gets sensitive. Workflows become dependent. Then the organization realizes the model is no longer a side feature. It is becoming part of the operating system of the business.

Cloud LLMs make sense for many use cases. They move fast, improve constantly, and reduce setup time. For experimentation, uneven demand, and frontier reasoning, cloud APIs are often the right answer.

But there is a

items.title: Every data platform diagram seems to start in the same place.
items.excerpt:
Every data platform diagram seems to start in the same place.

Sources on the left.
Pipelines in the middle.
Dashboards on the right.

It is technically correct, but it misses the part people actually care about.

Trust.

No executive has ever stopped me in a hallway to ask how many Spark jobs ran overnight. No operational leader has asked whether the data passed beautifully from Bronze to Silver to Gold. No clinician has ever celebrated a clean medallion architecture.

They ask a much simpler question:

“Can I trust this number?”

That is the real journey.

Not from raw to curated.
Not from lakehouse to warehouse.
Not from ingestion to visualization.

The real journey is from uncertainty to confidence.

Raw data says, “Something happened.”

Trusted data says, “We understand what happened, we agree

items.title: I read a post this week quoting Aravind Srinivas saying, “The model is no longer…
items.excerpt:
I read a post this week quoting Aravind Srinivas saying, “The model is no longer the product.”

My first reaction was disagreement.

Not because I think he’s wrong. I think the statement is incomplete.

For the past few years, we’ve been obsessed with comparing models. GPT vs. Claude. Gemini vs. Llama. Every benchmark tries to answer the same question: Which one is better?

But after spending the last year integrating AI into enterprise workflows, I don’t think that’s the question that matters most anymore.

In healthcare, nobody walks into my office asking for a better large language model.

They ask if we can reduce documentation time for clinicians. They ask if we can help analysts find answers faster. They ask if AI can eliminate repetitive work so people can spend more time solving real problems.

items.title: I’ve been trying to put a name to something that’s been bothering me for a while…
items.excerpt:
I’ve been trying to put a name to something that’s been bothering me for a while.

One of the biggest advances in modern analytics has been data lineage. We can trace where data originated, every transformation it went through, who touched it, and how it eventually found its way into a dashboard. Whether you’re working in Fabric, Databricks, or another modern platform, preserving that lineage has become an expected capability.

But I’ve started wondering if we’ve been preserving the wrong lineage.

What about decision lineage?

Data lineage tells us where the data came from. Decision lineage tells us why we trusted it.

Think about what really happens during an investigation. An analyst writes SQL, follows three bad hypotheses before finding the right one, asks an LLM to challenge assumptions, talks with operational leaders, discovers a workflow change

items.title: Why doesn’t this number match the other dashboard?
items.excerpt:
“Why doesn’t this number match the other dashboard?”

In healthcare analytics, that question rarely stays technical for long. A service-line leader may be planning staffing. Finance may be closing a month. An operational team may be deciding where access is breaking down for patients. When two dashboards disagree, the problem is not simply that a measure needs fixing. Trust starts to drain out of the decision.

Usually, nobody wrote obviously bad SQL. Two well-intentioned teams simply gave the same business term different meanings.

One revenue model is built at transaction grain; another comes from a summarized financial view. “Active patient” means one thing in an access dashboard and something else in a population-health analysis. Security is applied in different places. A measure sounds clear until someone asks whether it includes exclusions, reversals, late charges, or a particular reporting

items.title: I have been trying to make sense of Microsoft IQ without treating it as another …
items.excerpt:
I have been trying to make sense of Microsoft IQ without treating it as another collection of product names.

The simplest explanation is that Microsoft is trying to give agents shared enterprise context, rather than making every team rebuild it from scratch.

Microsoft Fabric is the governed data platform: lakehouse, warehouse, engineering, real-time analytics, data science, governance, and Power BI. In healthcare, it supports enterprise reporting, semantic models, executive dashboards, and operational analytics. It still has to coexist with Epic, Databricks, and the reality that no health system has only one source of truth.

Microsoft Foundry is where teams build, ground, evaluate, govern, and deploy AI apps and agents. Copilot Studio is where those agents can be created, customized, managed, and published into business workflows.

Microsoft IQ is the context layer those

items.title: I grew up in Southern Indiana, in one of those country places where “go outside”…
items.excerpt:
I grew up in Southern Indiana, in one of those country places where “go outside” was not a suggestion. It was the entire summer plan.

In the summer, I fished. In the fall, I hunted. In between, I explored every creek, field, barn, ditch, pond, and patch of woods that looked like it might contain crawdads, arrowheads, snakes, or trouble.

One hot afternoon, I was fishing with my brother Scott Barnes and some kids from down the country road. I had a Zebco rod, a worm, and the kind of confidence only a country kid can have when nobody has yet explained reality to him.

I decided I was going to catch the biggest bass in Southern Indiana.

Not a bass.

The bass.

So I put a nightcrawler on the hook that looked less like bait and more like it
